description: A sleek racing car with aerodynamic design. Perfect for beginners wanting to build their first vehicle.
funFact: The LEGO Group has produced over 4 billion tiny wheels, making them the world's largest tire manufacturer!

instructions.instruction: Build the base chassis using the 2×4 plates side by side
instructions.tip: Make sure they're flush

instructions.instruction: Add 2×4 bricks on each end for the body
Place 1×2 bricks along the sides for the doors
Add slopes at the front and back for the hood and trunk
Attach wheels to the underside
Add the windshield tile on top
instructions.tip: Use a transparent piece for the windshield

name: Classic Race Car

description: A big, beefy monster truck with oversized wheels and a raised chassis. Looks awesome on any shelf.
funFact: If you stacked all the LEGO bricks ever produced, they'd reach from Earth to the Moon ten times!

instructions.instruction: Start with the large 4×6 plate as the base
Build up the raised body with 2×2 and 2×4 bricks
Add 1×4 bricks along the sides for the frame rails
Use Technic bricks for the axle supports
Add slopes for the cab roof
instructions.tip: Angle them to create an aggressive look

instructions.instruction: Mount the large wheels

name: Monster Truck

description: A charming little house with a door, windows, and a peaked roof. A classic build that teaches fundamental techniques.
funFact: There are over 400 billion LEGO bricks in the world — about 62 for every person on Earth!

instructions.instruction: Place the 4×8 base plate — this is your foundation
Build the walls 3 bricks high, leaving gaps for the door and windows
Add slope bricks on each side to form the peaked roof
Add tile pieces for the door and window shutters
Top it off with plates along the roof ridge
instructions.tip: Use contrasting colors for the trim

name: Cozy Cottage

description: A tall, modern skyscraper with a sleek design. Stack it high and show off your architectural skills.

instructions.instruction: Start with two 4×4 plates stacked for a solid base
Build alternating rows of 2×2 bricks, offsetting each layer for the interlocking pattern
instructions.tip: Offset each row by one stud

instructions.instruction: Add 1×2 bricks as window columns between floors
Insert tile pieces as glass windows every 3 rows
Create floor separators with 2×4 plates
Top with slopes for the crown of the building

name: Skyscraper Tower

description: An adorable blocky dog with floppy ears. Simple enough for kids, cute enough for everyone.
funFact: LEGO dogs are the most popular animal build among LEGO fans worldwide!

instructions.instruction: Build the body with two 2×4 bricks stacked
Add 2×2 bricks for the legs on each end
Stack a 2×2 brick on top for the head
Add 1×2 bricks as floppy ears on each side
Place 1×1 black bricks as eyes
Add a black plate for the nose
Use a slope brick for the tail

name: Friendly Dog

description: A fearsome dragon with wings, a long tail, and a menacing stance. The crown jewel of any brick collection.
funFact: The largest LEGO dragon ever built used over 2 million bricks and was 5 meters long!

instructions.instruction: Build the body — a tapered shape using 2×4 and 2×2 bricks
Construct the neck by stacking offset 1×4 bricks upward at an angle
instructions.tip: Offset each brick by one stud for a curved look

instructions.instruction: Build the head with 2×2 bricks, add yellow eyes and red round bricks for nostrils
Create wings using plates and wedge pieces, attach with hinge pieces
Build the tail tapering from 2×2 down to 1×2 bricks
Add 4 legs using 2×2 bricks with slope feet
Add spine details with slopes along the back
instructions.tip: The spine ridges give it a fierce look!

name: Dragon

description: A small but mighty robot mech with articulated arms. Compact and satisfying to build.
funFact: LEGO Mindstorms was one of the first consumer robotics kits, released in 1998!

instructions.instruction: Build the torso with 2×4 bricks stacked 2 high
Attach 2×2 bricks as legs
Add plates to the feet for stability
Build arms with 1×2 bricks, two per arm
Place 2×2 brick on top for the head
instructions.tip: The head should overhang slightly for a cool look

instructions.instruction: Add round bricks as eyes and slopes as antennas

name: Mini Mech

description: A sleek interstellar fighter craft with swept wings and dual engines. Ready for galactic adventures.
funFact: The LEGO Classic Space theme, launched in 1978, is one of the most beloved LEGO themes ever.

instructions.instruction: Build the central fuselage with 2×4 plates and bricks
Add 1×4 bricks along each side for the wing roots
Attach wedge pieces as the swept wings
Build the cockpit with slopes and a transparent tile
Add round plates as engine nacelles
Place red round bricks as engine exhausts
instructions.tip: Use transparent red for a glow effect!

name: Star Fighter

description: A beautiful pixel art heart mosaic. Simple, colorful, and makes a great display piece or gift.

instructions.instruction: Place the base plate as your canvas
Following the pixel pattern, place red 1×1 plates to form the heart shape
instructions.tip: Start from the center and work outward

instructions.instruction: Fill the remaining spaces with white 1×1 plates

name: Pixel Art Heart

description: A lovely evergreen tree to add to any LEGO scene. Uses clever stacking of plates for a natural look.

instructions.instruction: Stack the brown 2×2 bricks for the trunk
Add the largest green plates at the bottom, rotated 45° from each other
Stack medium green plates, offset and rotated
Add small plates near the top
Top with a single 1×1 plate

name: Pine Tree

description: A functional phone stand you can actually use! Holds your phone at the perfect viewing angle.
funFact: This phone stand is actually functional — try it with your phone!

instructions.instruction: Place two 4×8 plates overlapping to create the wide base
Build the back support wall, 4 bricks high at an angle using 2×4 and 2×2 bricks
Add 1×4 bricks as the phone lip at the front edge
instructions.tip: This catches the bottom of your phone

instructions.instruction: Add slopes on the back for the angled support
Place tiles on top for a smooth finish

name: Phone Stand

description: A playable tic tac toe game with a board and X/O pieces. Build it, then play it!
funFact: LEGO board games were a short-lived but beloved product line from 2009-2013.

instructions.instruction: Place the base plate
Create the grid lines using black tiles — 2 horizontal and 2 vertical
Build 5 X pieces from blue 1×1 bricks arranged diagonally on small plates
The red round bricks serve as O pieces
instructions.tip: Now challenge someone to a game!

name: Tic Tac Toe Board

description: A cute little brick-built character with a big round head and tiny body. Pure personality in a small package.
funFact: The classic LEGO minifigure has the same permanent smile since 1978!

instructions.instruction: Place a 2×2 plate as the feet base
Stack 2 yellow 2×2 bricks for the body
Add blue 1×2 bricks on each side as arms
Place the last 2×2 yellow brick as the head
Add black 1×1 bricks as eyes
Add a red round brick as the nose and 1×2 plates as eyebrows

name: Brick Bot Buddy

description: A tiny desk and chair set, perfect for a minifigure office or study scene.

instructions.instruction: Place 4 single 1×1 bricks as desk legs
Set the 2×4 plate on top as the desk surface
Build the chair: place 4 more 1×1 bricks as legs topped with a 2×2 plate seat
Add a 1×2 brick behind the seat as the backrest, and a 2×4 plate on top for the desktop

name: Mini Desk & Chair

description: A rugged pickup truck with an open bed and chunky tires
funFact: The first LEGO vehicle set was released in 1958, just one year after the modern brick was patented!

instructions.instruction: Lay two 2×6 plates side by side to form the truck chassis
Attach four wheels to the underside of the chassis
Stack bricks on the front half to build the cab
Add slopes to the front for the hood and windshield
Place remaining bricks around the rear to form the truck bed walls

name: Pickup Truck

description: A charming little sailboat with a tall mast and colorful sail
funFact: A full-size LEGO sailboat with over 400,000 bricks was built and actually floated on water in 2018!

instructions.instruction: Build the hull using brown bricks stacked two high with plates on top
Stack three round bricks in the center to form the mast
Attach white slopes beside the mast to create the sail

name: Sailboat

description: A detailed helicopter with spinning rotors and a tail boom
funFact: The longest LEGO helicopter ever built measured over 7 feet and used more than 25,000 pieces!

instructions.instruction: Lay the two long plates as the fuselage base
Stack red bricks on the front half to form the cockpit body
Add slopes at the front for the nose and transparent tiles for the windshield
Attach technic beams crossing on top of a round plate to create the main rotor

name: Helicopter

description: A simple miniature bicycle with two wheels and handlebars
funFact: There are more bicycles in the world than cars — over 1 billion!

instructions.instruction: Use the technic beam as the bicycle frame
Attach a wheel to each end of the frame
Place round plates on top near the front as handlebars

name: Bicycle

description: A tall striped lighthouse perched on a rocky base with a glowing top
funFact: The oldest working lighthouse in the world, Tower of Hercules in Spain, has been guiding ships for nearly 2,000 years!

instructions.instruction: Place gray plates as the rocky base and add slopes around the edges
Alternate stacking white and red 2×2 bricks to build the tower
Place the yellow round brick on top as the lantern room light

name: Lighthouse

description: An imposing medieval castle tower with battlements, arrow slits, and a flag on top
funFact: The original LEGO Castle line launched in 1978 and remains one of the most beloved themes ever!

instructions.instruction: Lay two large plates as the tower foundation
Build four walls using 2×4 gray bricks, leaving gaps for arrow slits
Place arches across the front wall to form the entrance gate
Add 1×2 bricks in alternating positions along the top to create battlements
Attach a red tile on top of a 1×2 brick as a flag

name: Castle Tower

description: A classic Dutch-style windmill with spinning blades and a brick base
funFact: The Netherlands once had over 10,000 windmills — today about 1,000 still stand!

instructions.instruction: Stack tan bricks into a tapered tower shape, wider at the base
Add brown slopes around the top to form the cap roof
Attach the round brick to the front as the hub
Connect four technic beams to the hub in an X pattern as windmill blades

name: Windmill

description: A cozy elevated treehouse nestled in a brick-built tree with a ladder
funFact: The LEGO Ideas Treehouse set (#21318) uses botanical elements made from plant-based plastic sourced from sugarcane!

instructions.instruction: Stack brown 2×2 bricks into a tall trunk, about 8 bricks high
Place brown plates on top of the trunk as the treehouse floor
Build short tan brick walls on three sides and add yellow tiles as windows
Arrange green slopes all around the top and sides as leafy canopy

name: Treehouse

description: A sturdy arch bridge spanning a gap with road plates on top
funFact: The longest LEGO bridge ever built stretched over 104 feet at a 2019 event in Prague!

instructions.instruction: Build two pillars from 2×2 gray bricks, two bricks tall each
Place the three arches between the pillars to form the bridge span
Lay dark gray plates across the top of the arches as the road surface
Add white tiles along each edge as road markings and guard rails

name: Bridge

description: An adorable tuxedo penguin with a bright orange beak
funFact: Emperor penguins can dive to depths of over 1,800 feet — deeper than any other bird!

instructions.instruction: Stack two white bricks as the penguin's belly
Place black bricks on the sides and back to form the body and wings
Attach the orange slope at the front as the beak

name: Penguin

description: A tall giraffe with a long spotted neck and four sturdy legs
funFact: A giraffe's legs alone are taller than most adult humans — about 6 feet long!

instructions.instruction: Use two 2×4 yellow bricks to build the giraffe's body
Attach four orange 1×2 bricks underneath as the four legs
Stack six 2×2 yellow bricks vertically at the front to create the long neck
Place two brown round bricks on top as little ossicone horns

name: Giraffe

description: A colorful butterfly with symmetrical patterned wings spread wide
funFact: Butterflies taste with their feet and can detect food just by landing on it!

instructions.instruction: Place the black brick vertically as the butterfly's body
Attach purple plates on each side as the upper wings
Add pink plates below the purple ones as the lower wings
Place yellow round plates on the wings for decorative spots

name: Butterfly

description: A sleek gray shark with a pointed nose and menacing open jaw
funFact: Sharks have been around for over 400 million years — they predate dinosaurs and even trees!

instructions.instruction: Lay white plates as the shark's underside belly
Stack gray bricks on top to build the main body
Attach gray slopes at the front to form the pointed snout
Place gray tiles angled on top and sides as the dorsal and pectoral fins

name: Shark

description: A towering humanoid battle mech armed with cannons and heavy armor plating
funFact: The Japanese tradition of giant mech robots (mecha) started with the manga Mazinger Z in 1972!

instructions.instruction: Build the torso using 2×4 dark gray bricks with plates as shoulder mounts
instructions.piecesUsed: 3× Dark Gray Brick 2×4, 2× Dark Gray Plate 2×6

instructions.instruction: Attach technic beams as the two arms and two legs, angled outward
Stack 2×2 bricks at the bottom of each leg for armored feet
Add red slopes on the shoulders as armor plating and yellow rounds as cannon barrels
Place the remaining bricks on top as the cockpit head module

name: Battle Mech

description: A friendly little helper robot with big eyes and articulated arms
funFact: The word 'robot' comes from the Czech word 'robota', meaning forced labor, coined in a 1920 play!

instructions.instruction: Place the gray plate as the base and stack two light blue bricks as the body
Add the third brick on top as the head and attach white rounds as eyes
Attach 1×2 bricks on each side as the robot's arms

name: Helper Bot

description: A quadcopter drone with four rotors and a camera gimbal underneath
funFact: The first consumer drone with a camera, the Parrot AR.Drone, was announced at CES in 2010!

instructions.instruction: Start with the white plate as the central body frame
Attach four technic beams radiating outward from each corner as arms
Place a round plate at the end of each arm as the rotor housings
Add the transparent tile underneath the center as the camera lens

name: Drone

description: A classic pointed rocket with fins, a porthole, and flame exhaust at the base
funFact: The Saturn V rocket used to reach the Moon stood 363 feet tall — about the height of a 36-story building!

instructions.instruction: Place the dark gray plate as the base and add orange rounds underneath as exhaust flames
Stack white bricks six high to form the rocket fuselage
Attach the transparent round plate on one side as the porthole window
Place four red slopes at the top to form the pointed nose cone

name: Rocket Ship

description: A retro flying saucer with a domed cockpit and circular ring of lights
funFact: The term 'flying saucer' was coined by a journalist in 1947 who misquoted pilot Kenneth Arnold's description!

instructions.instruction: Stack the two large round plates together as the saucer disc
Place small dark gray plates on top as the cockpit base structure
Add the transparent dome on top as the cockpit canopy
Attach lime tiles around the rim of the saucer as glowing lights

name: Flying Saucer

description: A modular orbital space station with solar panels, docking ports, and a central habitat module
funFact: The International Space Station is the most expensive object ever built, costing over $150 billion!

instructions.instruction: Connect four white 2×6 bricks end-to-end to form the central habitat cylinder
Attach two technic beams through the center, extending outward as the truss structure
Mount dark blue plates at each end of the truss beams as solar panel arrays
Add white round bricks at each end of the habitat as docking port modules
Place transparent tiles along the habitat module as observation windows and add the center plate as a radiator

name: Space Station

description: A colorful flat mosaic flower built entirely from plates and tiles. Great for wall art.
funFact: LEGO mosaics became so popular that LEGO released an entire Art theme in 2020!

instructions.instruction: Arrange yellow plates in a 2×2 cluster for the flower center
Surround the center with red plates as petals
Attach green 1×2 plates downward for the stem
Place white tiles around the edges to frame the mosaic

name: Mosaic Flower

description: A tall, twisting tower that mixes slopes, arches, and bricks in contrasting colors. A challenging artistic build.
funFact: Professional LEGO artists can earn over $100,000 a year building large-scale sculptures!

instructions.instruction: Place the 4×4 dark gray plate as a stable base
Stack black 2×2 bricks, rotating each 45° conceptually for a spiral effect
instructions.tip: Offset each layer by half a stud

instructions.instruction: Insert red arches on opposite sides halfway up the tower
Add white slopes at the top angled outward for a crown effect
Cap with yellow round bricks as decorative finials

name: Abstract Tower

description: A miniature bonsai tree with a brown trunk and green leafy canopy on a tan base plate.
funFact: LEGO released a real Bonsai Tree set (#10281) in 2021 with swappable cherry blossoms!

instructions.instruction: Place the tan 4×4 plate as the pot base
Stack brown 1×2 bricks in a zigzag to form the trunk
instructions.tip: Offset each brick slightly for a natural look

instructions.instruction: Attach green 2×2 plates around the top as the canopy base
Add dark green round bricks on top for leafy clusters

name: Bonsai Tree

description: A cute little cactus in a pot. Just a few green pieces and you're done!
funFact: The saguaro cactus can live over 200 years — your LEGO one will last even longer!

instructions.instruction: Place the brown round brick as the pot
Stack three green 2×2 bricks on top for the main trunk
Attach 1×2 green bricks on each side as arms
Add tiny yellow plates as flowers on top

name: Cactus

description: A whimsical toadstool with a red spotted cap and white stem.
funFact: The fly agaric mushroom's red and white pattern inspired the mushrooms in Super Mario Bros!

instructions.instruction: Place the green 2×4 plate as the grassy base
Stack two white 2×2 bricks for the stem
Arrange four red slopes pointing outward to form the cap
Place white round 1×1 pieces on the cap as spots

name: Mushroom

description: A functional spinning top that actually spins! Uses a Technic axle through layered plates.
funFact: LEGO spinning tops can reach over 3,000 RPM when launched with a Technic string launcher!

instructions.instruction: Place the 4×4 plate as the top's body
Stack 2×2 plates on center for weight
Add slopes around the edges angled downward
instructions.tip: Point them down for the spinning shape

instructions.instruction: Push the Technic pin through the center hole as the axle

name: Spinning Top

description: A working mini catapult that can launch small bricks across the table using a Technic lever arm.
funFact: Medieval trebuchets could hurl 300-pound stones over 900 feet!

instructions.instruction: Place the 4×6 dark gray plate as the base
Build two brown 2×4 brick pillars on each side for the fulcrum
Rest the Technic beam across the pillars as the lever arm
Add gray bricks as a stopper on one end
Attach a 2×2 plate bucket on the launch end to hold ammo

name: Catapult

description: A buildable die with dots on all six faces. Roll it for board game night!
funFact: The opposite sides of a standard die always add up to 7!

instructions.instruction: Place a 2×2 plate, then stack two 2×2 white bricks on it
Add another 2×2 plate, then two more bricks to complete the cube
Cap the top with a 2×2 tile for a smooth face
Place black round dots on the visible faces to represent numbers 1–6
instructions.tip: Use 1 on top, 6 on bottom, etc.

name: Dice

description: A playable tic-tac-toe set with a grid and colored markers for X and O.
funFact: Tic-tac-toe has 255,168 possible games but only 138 unique ending positions!

instructions.instruction: Place the 6×6 gray plate as the board
Lay two black 1×4 tiles vertically to divide into 3 columns
Lay two more black 1×4 tiles horizontally to create the grid
Use red round pieces for O and blue plates for X markers
instructions.tip: Stack blue plates in an X shape for each marker

name: Tic-Tac-Toe Deluxe

description: A brave brick-built knight with a shield and sword, ready for castle defense.
funFact: The first LEGO Castle set was released in 1978 and is one of the longest-running themes!

instructions.instruction: Stack two gray 2×2 bricks for the body armor
Add a third 2×2 brick on top as the head, with black round eyes
Place dark gray slopes on top as the helmet visor
Attach 1×4 plates on each side — one as a sword, one as a shield arm
Add blue tiles on the chest as a coat of arms

name: Knight

description: A mystical wizard with a pointy hat and a magic staff. Easy but enchanting.
funFact: LEGO minifigure wizards have appeared in over 30 different sets since 1993!

instructions.instruction: Stack two purple 2×2 bricks for the robed body
Place the purple slope on top as the pointy wizard hat
Add yellow round pieces as glowing eyes
Attach the brown 1×4 plate to the side as a magic staff

name: Wizard

description: A swashbuckling pirate with an eye patch and a cutlass. Arrr!
funFact: LEGO Pirates was launched in 1989 and featured the first minifigure with a hook hand!

instructions.instruction: Stack two red 2×2 bricks for the pirate's coat
Place the tan 2×2 brick on top as the head
Add a black tile on one eye as the eye patch, white round on the other
Place two black tiles on top as the hat
Attach the gray 1×4 plate to one side as the cutlass

name: Pirate

description: A sturdy miniature desk with drawer detail and a smooth top surface.
funFact: The most expensive desk ever sold was a Badminton Cabinet — it went for $36 million!

instructions.instruction: Place four 1×1 bricks as table legs in a rectangle
Set the 4×6 plate on top as the desk surface
Add a 2×4 brick underneath as the drawer block
Place tan tiles on the drawer front as drawer handles

name: Desk

description: A miniature bookshelf with colorful book spines. Perfect for a mini room scene.
funFact: The Library of Congress holds over 170 million items — you'd need a LOT of LEGO bookshelves!

instructions.instruction: Place a 1×4 brown plate as the bottom shelf
Add 1×4 brown bricks on each side and one in the middle as dividers
Place 1×4 plates on top of the bricks for upper shelves
Stand colored tiles upright on the shelves as books

name: Bookshelf

description: A simple single chair. The most basic piece of LEGO furniture.
funFact: The world's most expensive chair is the Dragons Chair, sold for $28 million at auction!

instructions.instruction: Place four 1×1 bricks in a square as chair legs
Set the 2×2 plate on top as the seat
Add the 1×2 brick at the back of the seat as the backrest

name: Chair

description: A delicate snowflake built from white plates and tiles. Hang it anywhere for winter vibes.
funFact: No two real snowflakes are identical — but LEGO lets you build the same one every time!

instructions.instruction: Cross three 1×4 plates at their centers to form a six-armed star
Attach 1×2 plates at the end of each arm as branches
Place light blue round pieces at each branch tip
Add a transparent tile at the center for sparkle

name: Snowflake Ornament

description: A brick-built picture frame sized for a tiny photo or drawing. Simple and sweet.
funFact: The first picture frame patent was filed in 1898, but people have been framing art since ancient Egypt!

instructions.instruction: Lay down the white 4×4 tile as the picture area
Place 1×6 plates along the top and bottom edges
Place 1×4 plates along the left and right sides to complete the frame

name: Picture Frame

description: A golden trophy cup on a dark base. Award it to the best builder in the house!
funFact: The FIFA World Cup trophy is made of 18-karat gold and weighs 13.6 pounds!

instructions.instruction: Place the dark gray 2×4 plate as the base pedestal
Add the dark gray 1×2 brick centered on top as the stem
Stack two yellow 2×2 bricks on the stem for the cup body
Add inverted yellow slopes on each side as the cup handles
Place yellow 1×2 plates on top as the cup rim

name: Trophy

description: A classic knight's longsword with a crossguard and pommel. Display it or arm your minifigure army!
funFact: Medieval swords typically weighed only 2-4 pounds — much lighter than movies suggest!

instructions.instruction: Stack two 1×6 plates for the blade
Add 1×2 plates sideways for the crossguard
Attach 2×2 plate as the handle
Top with a 1×1 tile as the pommel

name: Medieval Sword

description: A round Viking shield with boss detail. Perfect companion to the Medieval Sword.
funFact: Viking shields were typically made from linden wood and were about 80-90cm in diameter.

instructions.instruction: Place the 4×4 plate as the shield body
Round out with 2×2 plates on edges
Add the 1×1 brick as the boss center
Top with decorative tile

name: Viking Shield

description: A classic deck-mounted cannon with wheels. Fire away, matey!
funFact: Naval cannons in the 1700s could fire a 32-pound ball over a mile!

instructions.instruction: Build the carriage from the 2×6 plate base
Add wheels to each side of the carriage
Stack 2×4 bricks for the barrel
Cap with 2×2 bricks and a slope for the muzzle

name: Pirate Cannon

description: A display stand with a recurve bow and quiver of arrows.
funFact: English longbows could shoot arrows over 300 yards at the Battle of Agincourt.

instructions.instruction: Build the display base with 4×4 plate
Create the bow limbs with 1×6 bricks and slopes
Build the quiver from 1×1 bricks
Add arrow shafts with 1×4 plates

name: Bow and Arrow Stand

description: A working-style medieval catapult with a swing arm and bucket.
funFact: Real trebuchets could hurl 300-pound projectiles over 300 meters!

instructions.instruction: Build the base frame on the 4×8 plate
Build uprights with 2×4 bricks on each side
Add the Technic beam as the swing arm through the uprights
Build the bucket from 2×2 bricks and plates
Add wheels for mobility

name: Catapult Siege Engine

description: A futuristic sci-fi blaster with a scope and energy cell.
funFact: The first laser was built in 1960 using a synthetic ruby crystal.

instructions.instruction: Stack 1×4 bricks for the barrel
Add 1×2 bricks for the grip
Place the transparent tile as the scope lens
Add slope at the back for the stock

name: Laser Blaster

description: A double-headed battle axe worthy of a dwarven warrior.
funFact: Viking battle axes could have heads up to 18 inches long.

instructions.instruction: Use the 1×6 plate as the handle
Attach slopes on each side as the axe heads
Add a 1×2 plate at the base of the handle

name: Battle Axe

description: A medieval crossbow with stirrup and bolt groove.
funFact: Medieval crossbows could penetrate plate armor at close range.

instructions.instruction: Lay the 1×8 plate as the tiller (stock)
Attach 1×4 plates at the front forming the prod (bow)
Add slopes for the curved bow tips
Place 1×1 bricks as the trigger mechanism

name: Crossbow

description: A hefty warhammer with a spiked head. Great for display.
funFact: Warhammers became popular because they could dent armor that swords couldn't cut.

instructions.instruction: Use the 1×6 plate as the shaft
Place the 2×2 brick at the top as the hammer head
Add 1×1 bricks on sides as spikes

name: Warhammer

description: A three-pronged trident fit for Poseidon himself.
funFact: Tridents were historically used more for fishing than for combat.

instructions.instruction: Use the 1×8 plate as the shaft
Attach three 1×4 plates at the top as prongs
Add 1×2 brick as the grip wrap

name: Trident

description: A wizard's staff with a glowing crystal on top.
funFact: The word 'wizard' comes from the Middle English 'wysard' meaning 'wise man'.

instructions.instruction: Use the 1×8 plate as the staff shaft
Add slopes as the head frame
Place the transparent 1×1 brick as the crystal

name: Magic Staff

description: A tall siege tower with a drop bridge and ladder.
funFact: Medieval siege towers could be over 40 feet tall and take weeks to construct.

instructions.instruction: Build the wheeled base from 6×6 plates
Add wheels on each corner
Build up walls with 1×6 bricks, leaving the front open
Add floors at intervals using 4×6 plates
Build the drop bridge at the top with 1×4 bricks

name: Siege Tower

description: A display stand with three different shields — round, kite, and heater.
funFact: The kite shield was developed by the Normans and was famously depicted in the Bayeux Tapestry.

instructions.instruction: Build the display base with the 6×8 plate
Build three shield stands using 1×2 bricks
Create round shield from 2×2 plates and slopes
Create kite shield from 2×4 plates and slopes
Create heater shield from remaining plates

name: Shield Wall Display

description: A Roman-style bolt thrower with torsion arms.
funFact: Roman ballistae could launch bolts with enough force to pin two soldiers together.

instructions.instruction: Build the frame on the 4×6 plate
Add 1×4 brick uprights on each side
Mount Technic beams as the torsion arms
Add the bolt rail with the 1×6 plate
Add optional wheels

name: Ballista

description: A small fortress wall with two mounted cannons.
funFact: Star forts with angled walls were designed specifically to deflect cannonballs.

instructions.instruction: Lay the 8×8 plate as the foundation
Build walls with 1×8 bricks, four rows high
Add crenellations with slopes
Build two cannons from 2×4 and 2×2 dark gray bricks
Mount cannons on wheeled carriages

name: Cannon Fortress

description: A low-profile F1 racer with front and rear wings.
funFact: An F1 car generates enough downforce to drive upside-down on a ceiling at speed.

instructions.instruction: Build the nose cone with 2×4 plates and slopes
Extend the body with the 2×8 plate
Add front wing from 4×4 plate
Add rear wing from 4×4 plate and slope
Mount all four wheels
Add windshield tile to cockpit

name: Formula 1 Car

description: A classic red fire truck with an extending ladder.
funFact: The first motorized fire engine was built in 1903 by the Knox Automobile Company.

instructions.instruction: Build the chassis on the 4×8 plate
Add 2×4 bricks for the cab and body
Build the cab front with 2×2 bricks
Create the ladder from 1×8 plates
Add lights and wheels

name: Fire Truck

description: A sleek passenger jet with swept wings and tail.
funFact: Commercial jets cruise at about 35,000 feet where the air temperature is -60°F.

instructions.instruction: Build the fuselage from 2×8 bricks
Add wings using 4×8 plates on each side
Build the nose with 2×4 slopes
Add engines under wings with 2×4 blue bricks
